chunk_summary: Catheter occlusion and thrombosis in parenteral nutrition require systematic evaluation, early escalation, and multidisciplinary management to prevent complications like hypoglycemia and loss of venous access.
learning_objectives: Identify mechanical, intraluminal, and extraluminal causes of catheter occlusion.
Understand the role of thrombolytic dwell therapy and declogging protocols in occlusion management.
Recognize symptoms of catheter-associated thrombosis and appropriate diagnostic imaging techniques.
Outline prevention strategies for catheter occlusion and thrombosis in parenteral nutrition.
key_facts: Catheter occlusion can lead to hypoglycemia, underfeeding, and loss of venous access.
Systematic evaluation includes checking for mechanical causes, intraluminal obstruction, and extraluminal thrombosis.
Thrombolytic dwell therapy is commonly used for thrombotic occlusion, with protocols tailored to institutions.
Catheter-associated thrombosis symptoms include limb/neck swelling, pain, erythema, and respiratory symptoms.
Diagnosis of thrombosis is typically performed via ultrasound, with further imaging based on symptoms.
Prevention includes using the smallest appropriate catheter, minimizing lumens, and maintaining strict aseptic technique.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Never forcefully flush an occluded catheter, because high pressure can rupture the line or dislodge clot.
document_key_topics: Recognition and diagnosis of catheter-associated thrombosis
